t: DSM-5-TR — "with predominant pain" and severity specifiers.
ICD-11 — 'Bodily Distress Disorder' — close to the European 'bodily distress syndrome' concept (Fink P.); severity (light, moderate, severe).
German S3 Guideline (Funktionelle Körperbeschwerden, 2018) — biopsychosocial approach.
Medical assessment — exclusion of somatic illness (but avoid excessive testing).
Illness preoccupation dominant; somatic symptoms minimal.
Clinical and laboratory confirmation; however, somatic symptom disorder may be comorbid.
One-time comprehensive medical examination; then minimal repetition.
— First medical aid physician center, psychiatrist/psychologist adjunct.
Single physician model (single physician approach) — all complaints are managed through that physician.
CBT — cognitive restructuring, reduction of catastrophic interpretation of bodily sensations, behavioral activation. Evidence: Kleinstäuber M. Cochrane 2014.
SSRI — for comorbid MDD/anxiety; duloxetine, amitriptyline when pain is dominant.
Structured limited checking — once comprehensive examination of the patient, then limited 'reassurance'.
German S3 Guideline — integrate care at the first medical aid level.
NICE — no separate guideline for somatic symptom disorder; general principles.
Cognitive restructuring + behavioral intervention (avoidance reduction, exposure). Kleinstäuber M. Cochrane 2014.
— Structured visit; limited number of checks; sequential explanation.
— CBT-based brief intervention at first aid level; then specialized service.
CBT and a multidisciplinary approach lead to significant improvement; chronic course.
symptoms are real for the patient; somatic disorder is a clinical condition, not simulation.
Myth 2: "Saying 'nothing is wrong' after a medical exam will reassure the patient"
Negative reassurance is short-term; maintains long-term anxiety. Structured explanation and biopsychosocial model are superior.
Myth 3: "Only a prolonged medical investigation will reveal the diagnosis"
repeated checking increases somatic focus and costs; one-time comprehensive assessment, then limited.
Myth 4: "Somatic symptom disorder is only a 'mental illness'"
biopsychosocial disorder; biological components (sensory sensitivity, gut-brain axis) are real.
Myth 5: "Antidepressant is the 'treatment' for somatic symptom disorder"
SSRI for comorbid symptoms; CBT first-line.
Kleinstäuber M. et al. Pharmacological interventions for somatoform disorders in adults. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2014;(11):CD010628.
Henningsen P. et al. Management of somatic symptom disorder. Dialogues Clin Neurosci 2018;20(1):23–31.
Schaefert R. et al. Non-specific, functional, and somatoform bodily complaints. Dtsch Arztebl Int 2012;109(47):803–813.
